item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Late to the party on this one, but I read it a few weeks ago and it’s been stuck in my head.

A good friend of mine once said “beware what thou doeth ironically, lest thou begin to do it ronically.” I’m giving this SCP a -1 for that reason, despite the fact that this is a genuinely good piece of writing. I feel I should clarify that since the article is at >400, this is more of a token personal -1 than a “this shouldn’t be on the site -1.”

This article is very well-crafted. A SCP that cleverly satirizes and condemns pickup artist culture, with some innovative elements of body and psychological horror. The prose is vivid, juxtaposing classic SCP clinical-tone with conversational English in a distinctly unsettling way. That can sometimes feel forced and jarring, but here that uneasy contrast works.

For me, though, this article oversteps the bounds of that kind of serious, cutting satire, and ends up in more gratuitous territory of the sort of sexual horror that I can’t stand. Fiction does not have to be happy and feel-good, there’s plenty of great writing that works based on sorrow or horror or other negative reactions in the reader. But this went too far for my taste.

The conclusion of the article lost me. I went from that sort of fascinated horror into a sort of very unpleasant dread, one that killed the more meaningful horror of the SCP. I see why this article appeals to a lot of people, and I understand why you wrote it, but I personally would have been happier if I’d never read it. That’s a guaranteed -1 for me.

That being said, this is a good article. I just hope I don’t stumble across something like this too often on the wiki.

item.title: Re: Qualified upvote
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the comment. I've responded in a bit more detail to Monteparnas upthread. I'd only add here that I will claim no credit for the metatextual benefits of flat characters that you've mentioned - that was not intentional, as I would prefer audience sympathy for them, and their limited dimensions are more the result of the pacing and medium/format. Still, if that gets your upvote, I certainly won't complain!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is actually really good critique, and deserves a response. This is probably less defensive than it will end up sounding, as I think that this is all valid, and I like discussing the decisions behind the work.

Structure
– hide block

I messed around with the order of the sections a lot in the drafting process, and I am pretty happy with where they are at the moment. You're correct that a properly structured database entry would have the full description at the start, but my concern is that this would undermine the power of the reveals as the story goes on - the phenomenon is less creepy than the -1 entities, which are less creepy than the book, which is less creepy than the logs. Re-ordering would mean that you know about the -1 entities and the book before reading anything from Cooper's notes, which might make readers impatient for the arrival of the "good bits".

Characters
– hide block

You're correct that Rey is a stereotype, and while it is not intended offensively, I can see how it might be so, and I apologise for that. Any suggestions on how to improve things there, let me know. However Rey has (within the small amount of text dedicated to him) a little bit of complexity. He is extremely good at his job, and at getting information. He is correct (and Cooper is wrong) that they should get out of the tunnels sooner. And ultimately I'm trying to use the stereotype as a shorthand way of keeping the reader guessing - what is Rey's deal really?

I've actually written a bit more from Rey's perspective in some (semi-related prequel) tales, starting with Tooth and Claw. Hopefully they provide him with a bit more substance.

As for Cooper, her "voice" is intended to be detached, especially in the notes. My image of her is extremely work-focused and efficient, personable but there to get the job done. I'm also trying to play with the audience in the notes, because I don't reveal that Riley Cooper is a woman until afterwards - so she really is "there as much as a male agent".

I worry that adding much more from the characters themselves, while it would help build up audience sympathy for them (which is very important, as you noted), would undermine my goals for misdirecting the audience, as well as slowing down the pace of the skip.

Language
– hide block

You got me on this one, for sure. Almost every SCP log has to flirt with the boundary between clinical tone and vivid description, and I have cheated in this one, erring on the side of description. It definitely won't work for everyone, and I'm sorry it was immersion-breaking. As an aside, I absolutely agree with your advice for drafting logs - I always try to picture the action, and then think about the clinical description for that action.

item.title: Qualified upvote
item.content:encoded:
The intricate fuckiness of the premise and the simple fact that the format worked for me, as much as I may feel—along lines similar to what Monteparnas described under their "The description is broken" spoiler—that it shouldn't have, leaves me with little choice but to upvote.

But Monteparnas' other criticism, that Rey is a razor-thin Latino/Hispanic stereotype and Cooper is only slightly more substantial, isn't something regarding which I can comfortably say "but it works!"

I think it gets my upvote in spite of this marked weakness because the poor characterization becomes a self-referential element: the work itself only offers characters that basically conform to the terribly warped and flattened concept of humanity that is central to—and very successfully described/satirized/evoked within—the story. This (however accidentally) reinforces and expands the skip's particular flavor of alienated dread, and helped me reach the end of writing my comment here still feeling deeply unsettled by the whole thing.

So—amazing/horrifying concept; execution that is largely brilliant but downright bad in one area, but the bad ends up being metatextually brilliant. Shit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I upvoted as soon as it was revealed that this article is basically a skewering of pick up "artistry." the "plastic and unreal" bit made me think of 847 … I sort of wondered… but it wasn't until the mention of the book that I knew for certain.

what makes me sad is that i can see PUA or incel types doing this absolutely without hesitation, jumping at the chance. they would want to. they don't care about women's agency. they don't respect or see women as people. saying no is a personal slight to them, not an exercise of autonomy. for that reason, it would have been nice to see Cooper emerge triumphant, or at the very least to see the Josep dude, or somebody doing this kind of stuff, get busted. that said, the idea of the foundation making catching these men a low priority is depressingly realistic.

I wonder why/ how that one woman died…

creepy AND depressing. woo!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is excellent, obviously, but there's a couple things I like that I didn't see mentioned in the comments.
1) The title of the SCP, "The More You Know". If you remember NBC and their "The More You Know" segments, you'll see a shooting star when that segment's over. Nice tie to Fifthism there.
2) The pick-up artist's name is Enigma. I remember in the last decade on VH1, there was a show called, of course, The Pick-Up Artist. The host teaches tools how to pick up women. The host's name? Mystery.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmmm. They recovered Agent Cooper's camera when someone placed it on the top of the staits. But what about Agent Rey's camera? There is some footage from it in the logs when Cooper was captured but no mention of it being recovered.

item.title: Gahhhh
item.content:encoded: Holy mother of a thousand young. That was horrifying. I can't think of a time I had a more visceral reaction to anything other than The Red Knife, the horror-coral, and the chess-playing twins. Christ. The river of fat and the ending, ughh. Well done. +1 easy. Gahhh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First of all, I liked the general idea a lot, and most of the plot. The book, the homunculi, the criminal behind the outbreak. The Containment was on the spot and made good use of the MTFs.

But there are some big problems, and they made me downvote:

The description is broken
– hide block

It should be all in the Description section right away, what is a -1, what is a -2, and preferably pushing them to -2 and -3 and making the victims themselves -1 (since they are anomalous enough to warrant containment).

Not saying that just as a rule, but I really got confused and upset, and that affected my mood through the rest of the skip, I almost lost interest before I could really understand what was happening. This was not a mystery, to make myself clear, it was actually just confusing.

Characters that seem artificial
– hide block

Rey is a stereotype. He gets few lines, what seemed like a clever move to not show how little he was beyond a stereotype, but it was clear to me. On top of that, as a Brazilian I'm from one of the people that get that stereotype a lot on english fiction, so it was really annoying.

Agent Cooper is too detached, like she was not there. It isn't so weird on the actual exploration, since there she is acting and seeing more than commenting, but if her notes weren't meant to relate to her or otherwise contextualize her person, they could be shortened to a brief description of events.

In the end, I couldn't be moved to care about the destinies of either of them. I actually want more to learn about the sicko who brainwashed and murdered those women.

Sometimes the tone gets way off
– hide block

During the Description and most addenda it is hard to put my finger on it, but in general it seems that the text could be reasonably more concise and clear. But it is worse during the Exploration Log, with passages like "carved into abstract organic shapes" (what does it mean?), and "records the wet, sucking noises of the underground river" (informal, abstract adjectives).

My disbelief kicked in quickly during the log because of this. The agents are certainly exempt of being clinical during an exploration, but the person transcribing the video isn't. So, by the end, I was upset, didn't cared about the agents and lost immersion.

What could be done, in my opinion
– hide block

You don't have to do anything, of course. This article is very successful and I'm talking about my personal experience, only. But maybe those changes can improve it, since they don't touch in the actual story being told. Or inform you changes of your own to achieve that.

1- Get to move the description in its entirety to the beginning of the session, and rewrite passages to trim down excess, make it more concise. When the notes begin, all the description should be already laid down. The excerpts of the book are pretty good already.

2- On the exploration log you could try picture it on your mind like stills from a movie, or even draw then if you're able to, and describe only what you see as it is, exactly and avoiding metaphors.

3- Put some personal observations in there for the characters, specially in Cooper's notes, since they are personal notes. She doesn't need to be weak or fearful in any way, but just be there as much as a male agent that happened upon a situation where he's a potential target would be there. Rey can be more present through some notes of his own, or with more action and lines for both during the log, but that would be trickier in my opinion.

item.title: Ohwee
item.content:encoded:
“Hey jimmy you gotta read this book, I found it on the braincel subreddit! It’ll teach you how to get girls! All you have to do is make a spine homonculus, Get the girls, mess up their brains beyond recognition, throw them into a river of fat, then bam! That’s an epic gamer move right there.”

+1 for the tone, imagery, and that *damn* pit. This would work wonderfully as some kind of short film.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Been a long time since I read a scip that hit me that hard. In a way it left me feeling much the same way as I did when I first saw the movie Seven. Horrified, revolted, but also blown away. Any story involving a persons personality being altered is generally unsettling, and also hard to pull off well. This manages that on a truly nasty level, the way the womens personalities and natures aren't so much lost as they are taken. It's a personal nope button I suppose, the loss of agency and free will and self. Fucked up, that.
Take your plus 1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd never finished reading this during the 3000 contest. I got the basic concept from comments and never bothered to come back and read further. Stupid of me. This is absolutely incredible, gut-churning and tense in equal turns. Little turns like the river of fat are inspired, and give it a flavor that goes beyond technical document. One of the best articles on the site, and criminally underrated.

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
I'm happy with the Fifthist link (particularly as it comes at a stage when hopefully that should be clear to the audience), and with the "In the Trenches" link, which I've moved down to the very last reference to Agent Cooper in the text.

As for the Barcelona Skyline, that was always intended to be an absolute Easter egg, so I would prefer to leave it unlinked.

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: Probably should put it near the end, since spoilers?

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: I've added a link to "In the Trenches with the Dead"

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: It's a series, hubbed at Antimemetics Division Hub. Riley appears in In the Trenches with the Dead and A Thin Dangerous Line (she is one of the protagonists and will continue to feature in these going forward).

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: Unless I'm missing something, I can't find that tale?

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
It's also worth noting that this line:

15 July 2016: Landed 16:15. Hot here - hotter than Boston was, and no breeze to speak of. Agent Rey met me at the hotel, offered to show me the city skyline. I said no, thought we'd get going, but he was in no hurry. Cultural thing, maybe? Shame if I was rude, but I could do with the sleep.

Is meant to be a reference to SCP-2728, as confirmed on psul's author page. That said, making the crosslink would sorta recontextualize the line. (And that's recontextualize would very much change the meaning of the line.) So psul should weigh in on it first, really.

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: FWIW, Riley Cooper appears in my "What the Dead Know" and Tomàs Rey will appear in the next installment.

item.title: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Edit: timer expired, edits made

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

This SCP definitely has some ties to Fifthism, which has a hub. I propose crosslinking to the hub for that purpose.

Editing this:

Its Schläfli symbol is {5/2 , 5}, a fact I have dropped into many a flirtatious conversation, to great effect.

To this:

Its Schläfli symbol is {5/2 , 5}, a fact I have dropped into many a flirtatious conversation, to great effect.

Editing this:

Agent Cooper is heard clearly only once, when she approaches the camera's position after approximately 20 hours.

To this:

[[Cooper] is heard clearly only once, when she approaches the camera's position after approximately 20 hours.

item.title: Regarding the "female horror" element...
item.content:encoded:
I agree it feels somewhat detatched. I think it lacks a personal response to the affected women's condition from a "healthy" woman's perspective. It's not enough that the families are horrified- that's taken for granted after you've read enough SCPs. Agent Cooper, who is our POV character, is very neutral towards the situation. I understand that you wanted to make her gender ambiguous at first, but as it stands she's more horrified by the hot weather than by the erasure of women's personhood (is that even a word?).
Having her express particular disgust at the women's condition, some nervousness of the possibility of this SCP affecting her as well (even in a strictly professional manner like using some kind of memetic warding before she walks into the tunnels- she does it before opening the book but not when putting herself in direct danger of being affected by something that obviously hunts women?).
I also find her dismissive attitude towards the book a little strange- IMHO a woman who has to deal with a case of brainwashed "Stepford wives" would have been more on edge about an unusually creepy PUA guide, even if it doesn't contain any cognitohazards at first glance. At the very least it's a significant lead in her investigation.
Otherwise I feel it was well written and really hit the fifthist vibe.

item.title: Re: Great at times
item.content:encoded:
I'm also curious as to what actually happens to the woman to cause that effect in the first place

I think that can be surmised based on putting together the hints across the article, as follows:

  • The book says to prepare the -1 instance, then: "… concentrate your thoughts on [the intended victim's] face, and particularly the inside of her mouth and throat. Soon you'll see your new friend scurry off…"
  • Agent Cooper has a very large -1 instance land directly on top of her. She later exhibits "party girl" symptoms (high sociability, agreeableness, lack of inhibition) together with screaming.
  • These are the symptoms described for SCP-3512 in the first place.

As such, it seems like each of the 9 affected women were attacked by (smaller) -1 instances, targeted by people who have read the book.

the foundation sending Cooper to a SCP that effects woman is kind of idiotic. It didn't help that I couldn't really tell the agents genders (at first).

I deliberately concealed that Agent Cooper's is a woman for as long as possible (hence the first name "Riley"), in part because I don't want her marked as a possible victim immediately, and in part because I want the reveal to re-contextualise Cooper's and Rey's initial interactions.

I see where you're coming from in relation to having Cooper investigate, but this phenomenon clearly doesn't affect *all* women (only 9 victims so far), and it might be possible that having a woman involved will help the investigation (eg because the victims or their families might talk to her in a way they wouldn't with a man). And of course, if Cooper hadn't been a woman, Oriol would not have approached her at the nightclub, so the Foundation would have known less than they do now.

item.title: Great at times
item.content:encoded:
Not an active cementer, but I enjoyed this SCP.
I have a few issues. For one, the foundation sending Cooper to a SCP that effects woman is kind of idiotic.
It didn't help that I couldn't really tell the agents genders (at first).

I'm kind of surprised how horrified people are at the effect, Objectification is bad, but, cmon, that's the part that scares you?

I'm also curious as to what actually happens to the woman to cause that effect in the first place, as Cooper is dragged into the fat and next we see her acting like…a party girl? idk.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In a way, reality has no catharsis. And sometime people get away with doing bad things. I think that's it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So can anyone explain this to me.. I did not quite get it..

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Deeply disturbing on many levels.

And each level is further below.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Deeply disturbing on many levels. Which I presume was the point. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
first of all, SCP-3512-2 hasn't even been mentioned in the article prior to this moment.

That is a very good point, YourBuddyBill. It's a product of having changed the order around several times during the drafting - previously the book extracts came first, then this section (with the designations reversed), but the book extracts are stronger material, so I wanted them to come later in the skip.

So how to solve it? I'm open to suggestions. Perhaps the simplest is to amend the text to read "described in the book designated as SCP-3512-2 (see addendum below)" - what do you think?

Other options include annotating the Investigation Notes to make clear that the book has this designation, but I think that might interrupt the flow too much.

Second of all, Chapter Twelve [18]?

Hopefully that becomes clear later - there are twelve chapters called "Chapter Twelve", so the Foundation needs a shorthand way of distinguishing between them. Chapter Twelve [18] is the eighteenth chapter of the book.

That said, my suggested amendment above removes the chapter reference, as I can understand how it's confusing at that stage of the skip's text.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-3512-1 is the collective designation for a category of objects or entities described in Chapter Twelve [18] of SCP-3512-2.

first of all, SCP-3512-2 hasn't even been mentioned in the article prior to this moment. Second of all, Chapter Twelve [18]?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Truth be told, I'm pleased that you didn't enjoy this. For one thing, that's a sign that you have a working moral compass. But also, I deliberately aimed to make this unpleasant - at least from the stage that I realised that was the destination which was true to the themes of the piece. So I wanted to push the horror beyond what I'd written before, and I definitely wanted to leave the reader without catharsis - the title is intended ironically.

That said, I'm not blind to the risks here. As I mentioned to thedeadlymoose on an earlier discussion page, I have some concerns about whether this crosses the line into being exploitative. The fact that you're clear about who the villains are (that is, the fact that I have sufficiently clearly identified them as villains such that you have also called them that) gives me some encouragement on that front.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know the scp foundation isn't meant to be all sunshine and rainbows but the downer ending is just too much here. These are villains so disgusting with motivations so disgusting that there's simply nothing fun, not even in the context of horror, in following a woman who ends up violated and rotting in a hole. Especially not when we all know there are whole communities of people who would read this and cheer for those villains.

Some scenarios NEED relief and catharsis for the reader, and especially in today's culture, this is one of them. I would upvote this if the agent made an escape and a more hideous fate befell one of these rapists rather than her, or even if this could be one of those few SCP's to get royally neutralized.

item.title: Re: :O
item.content:encoded: When we die, we will all become part of the "all-psul".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
HAH! I SAW IT! >:V

And trust me, it's too late. :B

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know this is a bit off topic, and a bit of a necropost, and that TL333s probably won't see this, but…

Don't let knowledge make you lose faith. Awareness of the problem that is /r/redpill means that we can avoid and/or counter it. The Foundation doesn't ignore anomalies, they contain and counter them (yes, I know that's cheesy, but it works so let me have that).

Also…those idiots are a small, small, small fraction of people. Less than 1/3 of the people in the US voted for Trump. He didn't win because his platform was more popular, he won because of a combination of apathy, willful ignorance, fear, desperation, and hatemongering. The kind of people who genuinely believe their rhetoric *even on redpill* aren't all of those subscribers. And that number is already a small fraction of the whole.

Don't let the vocal minority destroy your hope, man.

item.title: Re: +200!
item.content:encoded: I dont think my hand can handle that much.
Thank you very much to everyone who voted - I have about 17 million 3512-1 entities ready to give you a high five :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was fantastic; the technical polish was A+ — and the creep-vibes were extraordinary. Deciding to combine pick-up culture with existential body-horror and blood rituals was a particularly inspired touch. +100

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It was pretty effective, but then there's the question: Why did a double agent act so weird and essentially blow their cover, thus giving the Foundation more info about the anomaly?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Love it, feels distinctly Fifthist, with a focus on article progression in a way that unravels a seemingly benign object into deeper and more foul levels of horror than expected.

I'm a sucker for atmospheric skips, so don't be disheartened by people complaining about extraneous content. They said the same thing about mine but eh, I like exploration logs and immersive worlds.

plus to the 1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not sure how I feel about the permanence but I think that it'd be better to say that SCP-3512 does not revert to their old personalities over time. Forever changed, as opposed to forever unchanging.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoy someone pointing out how inherently disgusting pickup artist culture is, because I can only imagine that a pickup artist (with the ability to not go insane in this process, slash the ability to not care) would do this. For future reference people, how to "get women" is the same skill as making friends and engaging in small talk and building connections. There is no trick. Why? Because they're feckin' people damn it.

Anyways, legitimately creepy, and I am glad this didn't go into sexual imagery when it could have (would have been less scary and more what one might expect). Poor Barcelona women… +1

EDIT:

Landed 16:15. Hot here - hotter than Boston was, and no breeze to speak of. Agent Rey met me at the hotel, offered to show me the city skyline. I said no, thought we'd get going, but he was in no hurry. Cultural thing, maybe? Shame if I was rude, but I could do with the sleep.

...?

item.title: Re: :O
item.content:encoded: As the high priest of the church of psul, I am honored by your words

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks a bunch, moose - I'm very pleased that you like it.

I think the reason for making the personality changes permanent was primarily a mechanical writing issue - it seemed like the best way to finish off that paragraph, and of course making the change permanent is a simple way to emphasise the severity of the skip. Good point about it being a trope, though.

Something slightly struck me as off — something about the horror of this from the women's perspective being less present than I would expect (since it's theoretically the most horrifying thing here). Can't quite put my finger on it, though.

I would be quite interested in discussing this more, as it plays into a concern I had with the skip while I was writing it. I am a man, so I was very worried that this could be insufficiently grounded in real experience of this type of abuse, or even become exploitative. Your comment suggests that there might be something to that concern, and it would be good to explore that (if you or others are happy to) with a view to improving the skip and my writing generally.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks Hercules. Numerology has always been part of Fifthism (duh!): I wanted to add a line to "Stars may die in threes, but worlds die in fives", with "A twelve is nothing but a hollow shell for fives to hide in".

I couldn't find a spot for that line in the skip itself, but it is reflected in a couple of different places - and in the SCP number too!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry for the delay, Idletunage. Your guess is pretty much exactly correct, particularly:

somehow I feel like there just barely isn't enough to implicate him

I deliberately wanted to leave the question of Rey's involvement ambiguous, with the aim of leaving things unsettled at the end of the skip. As such, I don't really mind which way a reader interprets his actions. I certainly agree that he is unlikely to be "Enigma" or a similar leading player. My mild personal preference is that he is involved - that's the reason I added the interaction with his sister to the diary, as that recontextualises horribly if Rey is part of this.

Early drafts had Rey in a much more heroic role - he was the one that shot off the arms of the -1 that attacked Cooper, although he wasn't able to save her. But that felt like it was distracting from the horror - having him simply drop inexplicably out of the story was quicker, easier and (I think) more effective.

item.title: Re: :O
item.content:encoded: Thanks for being such a consistent cheerleader for this entry, Bluemorpho. Much appreciated!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This disgusts me and scares me in a very personal manner. The real life horror of objectification taken to an extreme is really well done!

item.title: ಠ_ಠ
item.content:encoded:
…Good lord, what the fuck?… Nice work.

In other news, this gives me an idea for a Vampire the Masquerade character concept… :I

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is quite possibly the scariest and most unnerving SCP on the site.

I love the buildup. From the moment that I realized Cooper was a woman I had a pretty good idea of what was going to happen to her, but the mounting dread as she gets further and further into it, the horror of what's actually happening to these women, the implied betrayal and the utterly horrifying ending really made it work for me. It pretty much takes every possible man-related fear and just runs with it in the most viscerally terrifying way.

In conclusion, I'm never going to a club again. 0_0

(Or sleeping for that matter)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Looking back, this was better than I gave it credit for. It struck the wrong chord with me emotionally, but that's neither fair nor useful criticism. I've upvoted this and all the other competing 3000s I'd originally passed up for the contest 's sake. Well-written, and well-answered.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, my guess is that he's somehow related to the phenomenon, like he's in on it (though maybe not the 'leading player', so to speak)—but somehow I feel like there just barely isn't enough to implicate him. Am I close?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's the actual current ranking, Bluemorpho is complaining that this isn't ranked higher.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know, I had literally the exact same list as you, but after rereading this and seeing all the little hints in it, I had to rank it above Tower.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
it makes me start wondering what happens when one arbitrarily irreversible change meets another arbitrarily irreversible change

Probably depends on what the sources are, and how irreversible they really are, in some cases I'd expect dissociative states or other signs of mental frission, in others rationalization and fusion of the two changes and in still more that there exists no record of anyone infected by SCP-3512 with 2/2140 clearance. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is really well done. The texture, the detail work, the impeccable writing. And the concept is a good one.

Something slightly struck me as off — something about the horror of this from the women's perspective being less present than I would expect (since it's theoretically the most horrifying thing here). Can't quite put my finger on it, though. It's still so well done that it's an easy upvote.

Very minor note: The fact that the personality changes are irreversible felt contrived for me. This trope seems to crop up quite a bit; it makes me start wondering what happens when one arbitrarily irreversible change meets another arbitrarily irreversible change.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1rst Place: Anantashesha

2nd Place: Red Reality

3rd Place: Tower

11th Place: The More You Know

the world is cruel

goddammit at least let red reality win

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Should this also have the Fifthist tag?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is important in that it has topical purpose. It doesn't just inspire fear; it does so for a reason.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I still love it. There aren't many skips I can think of that manage to be so inscrutable yet at the same time so tantalizing - that's a hard balance to strike. I also love the idea of geometry/numbers as a source of anomalous power (at least this is what I'm getting from this - that dodecahedron thing seems to me like it's the real anomaly here). I think this might be your best work yet.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Alright that's fucky enough to get me to be fascinated. Upvoted.

Wasn't initially hooked but found the real meat was in the last log. That was horrifying enough to stand on it's own if it had to.

But without the earlier logs from Agent Cooper about Rey that might not have left me speculating if Rey was in on 3000/part of it.
